data.title: Dell-IP Storage Fuels Growth And Protects Margins -- What Investors Should Know
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last coverage of Dell Technologies (DELL), I maintained a bullish view based on the company’s robust growth in the Infrastructure Solutions Group ("ISG") and the strategic actions taken to address weaknesses in both the storage business and the Client Solutions Group ("CSG").

I also highlighted the company’s low profit margins and its attractive valuation supported by its overall strong momentum in ISG.

In this article, I will explain why I continue to hold a bullish view. From Oct. 27, 2025, until the end of November, Dell’s stock price has decreased by about 17%, with concerns over a potential future margin reduction becoming the main issue for investors. The storage business line remains a key factor for further margin expansion, while the CSG segment continues to be my primary risk concern.

Higher Memory Prices Pressure Dell’s Margins

First, let me summarize the company’s current margin and profitability status. Dell’s gross margin stood at 20.7% in Q3 FY26, up compared to the previous quarter but down YoY. However, in terms of operating performance, the company reported a 7.8% margin in Q3 FY26, compared to 6% in Q2 FY26 and 7.1% in Q3 FY25, which signals that Dell achieved to reduce OpEx and benefit from the scaling of its operations — lower cost per unit. In absolute terms, Dell reported net income of $1.5 billion, up 32% vs. $1.17 billion in Q3 FY25. Although the company faces increased operating costs, it has managed to sustain and even expand its profitability.

Diving into the segments, I noticed that the main driver behind the company’s gross margin reduction is the significantly higher cost of goods sold (COGS) in the ISG segment. Specifically, COGS reached $13.16 billion in Q3 FY26, up about 62% YoY compared to $8.13 billion in Q3 FY25. The rest of the expenses remained at the same level for both segments.

The main reason for the increased COGS is the higher prices of DRAM and NAND memory, which are crucial hardware components for AI infrastructure workloads. These memories are a core part of the AI hardware stack, and in combination with the massive data center demand and the limited supply across the broader memory industry, they have pushed their prices significantly higher.

Notably, Samsung has raised its memory prices by 60% compared to September 2025, and a report from Counterpoint Research forecasts that DRAM prices will double from Q1’25 to the end of CY26, signaling that Dell will have to deal with elevated COGS for the coming quarters.

To summarize, Dell is facing gross margin pressure due to increased memory prices — primarily DRAM and NAND — which are crucial components in AI infrastructure. However, its profitability and operating margin remain strong thanks to the company’s disciplined expense management.

Servers Drive Growth But Storage (Could) Drive Margins

It is well known that revenue from the ISG segment has already exceeded the more traditional CSG. With a closer look at Q3’25 results, I notice that CSG revenue accounted for $12.1 billion, while ISG revenue was $11.36 billion. In Q3’26, CSG reported about $12.5 billion, while ISG reported $14.1 billion, which means a YoY growth of 3% and 35%, respectively.

The company has strong momentum in the AI infrastructure market, but although revenue growth is robust, its margins remain at relatively low levels, as mentioned above— but Dell continues to expand consistently. In my view, a key reason for this steady margin expansion is the reduction in expenses, mainly in SG&A, along with a hidden factor: Dell-IP storage.

Dell, like all companies, can only reduce its expenses to a limit; therefore, the company has to find other ways to expand its margins in the future. So, Dell has already found them.

Based on the company’s statement:

Our ISG operating income rate was up 360 basis points sequentially to 12.4% of revenue. This improvement was driven by mix of AI servers, sequential improvement in AI server margins and stronger profitability from storage…The mix continues to increase across our storage revenue dollars. The margins within the portfolio continue to improve…So I think PowerMax, PowerScale, PowerStore, ObjectScale and PowerFlex all growing. We've talked about PowerStore in 7 quarters of growth, 6 of those double digits

Let me explain. Dell-IP storage is a set of branded hardware—such as arrays and all-flash storage—and is usually combined with software for management, automation, backup, or cybersecurity purposes.

Using its Dell-IP storage set of hardware and software (or Dell-IP ecosystem) in AI infrastructure projects can benefit the company because the Dell-IP hardware are premium products compared to the market, according to the company. This gives the company space to maintain higher prices for these products, supporting the company’s margins.

The combination of Dell-IP storage hardware and the company’s strong momentum in the AI infrastructure market could drive further margin expansion. I believe Dell is well-positioned to grow its branded storage solutions through a strong ISG segment.

As an investor who already holds a position in DELL, I would like to see whether the company can continue to increase the share of revenue coming from the Dell-IP system. The positive news is that the company expects further growth in Dell-IP, based on the company’s CFO statement:

Look, we expect to continue to make progress. Our Q4 profit guidance is anchored again through the storage P&L. With the Dell-IP storage growth, we expect to make it 4 for 4 in terms of quarterly growth in that portfolio. That should allow us to grow at or likely slightly ahead of normal sequentials, which will allow us to see an uptick in our op inc rate sequentially also into Q4.

Valuation

In my valuation analysis, I consider the company’s momentum. Given that Dell shows strong momentum with a backlog of $18.4 billion, AI shipments of $5.6 billion, and new orders of $12.3 billion in Q3’26, this reflects the company’s high demand for its ISG services. Notably, Dell estimates AI shipments of $25 billion for FY26, a YoY increase of 150%.

The company’s valuation seems even more attractive following the recent dip. Traditional ratios, such as P/E, EV/Sales, and P/S, all indicate that Dell remains significantly undervalued compared to the sector.

In terms of growth, the company is expected to achieve double-digit revenue growth over the next two years. Additionally, Dell anticipates revenue growth of 17% for FY26, slightly above analysts’ estimate of 16.57%. In my view, Dell has the potential to exceed this expectation.

Lastly, while Dell’s gross margin remains below the sector average, its EBIT and net profit margins are higher than the sector, reflecting the company’s ability to control expenses and operate efficiently, despite price pressures from increased DRAM and NAND memory costs.

Overall, the company seems undervalued in my view, considering its growth momentum and the significant potential for margin expansion, mainly through the Dell-IP storage ecosystem. Low margins remain the main risk concern for analysts and investors; however, I believe Dell can manage them effectively. Investors should closely monitor the trend in storage revenue, as well as the company’s overall margin trajectory. That said, margins are not my main risk, but they are not the only one.

Risk Analysis

My main risk is not the low margins, because I believe Dell can increase them through support from the storage hardware and disciplined expense management. Especially considering that the “all-flash portion of the portfolio is growing double digits” for the second consecutive quarter — the flagship product of Dell’s branded storage ecosystem.

My main concern is the company’s traditional segment, the CSG, which includes branded hardware such as PCs, laptops, monitors, and general peripheral devices. The CSG segment remained almost flat in Q3’26, with a revenue growth of 3% YoY, but its operating income has been continuously decreasing over the last quarters. Overall, the segment has not contributed significantly to the company’s profitability.

In my view, Dell has to balance the growth of this segment without negatively impacting its segment margin and, consequently, the company’s overall profit margin. The company noted that the PC market is expected to remain and that it lost a portion of market share in the non-premium segment but increased its share in the commercial premium segment. I believe the company’s focus on premium products is strategic, as premium PCs and laptops have better margins than non-premium ones.

However, I see that the overall CSG segment remains weak and does not contribute — or may even negatively contribute — to the company’s overall profitability. Any change in demand for AI infrastructure could result in a significant slowdown in the ISG revenue segment, and CSG would not be able to support the company’s growth outlook.

Although the possibility of a slowdown in AI infrastructure is limited, given the broad AI market demand and the company’s strong backlog, investors should consider the company’s weak CSG segment.

Currently, there is an opportunity for Dell to boost its revenue from the end of Windows 10 support, with potentially 1 billion PCs as candidates for upgrade or replacement, as well as from AI PCs enhanced by small language models.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Dell remains an undervalued stock with impressive revenue growth, primarily in the ISG segment, while the CSG segment remains weak. Additionally, the company’s Dell-IP ecosystem could further boost margins, and investors should monitor the trajectory of the storage business line.

The risk of a weak CSG segment is offset by opportunities from replacement PCs and laptops due to the end of Windows 10 support, as well as from AI-enhanced PCs.

Overall, considering the company’s risk-reward profile, valuation based on growth and value metrics, and future growth prospects, Dell remains attractive in my view. Therefore, I maintain Dell’s stock rating as "Buy."

data.title: Catalyst: Firdapse Cliff Deferred, But Capital Allocation Still An Issue (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Today, I am revisiting Catalyst Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (CPRX). I last wrote about this company in September 2024. Things were looking bleak at that time. Catalyst’s flagship drug, FIRDAPSE, appeared to be on the verge of losing market exclusivity amid a legal battle with Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA).

Its other drugs, AGAMREE and FYCOMPA, faced competition from a few other branded and generic drugs in their respective markets. FYCOMPA, like FIRDAPSE, faced a patent cliff, too.

For these reasons, I sided with caution (Sell rating) and posited that its stock “very much relies on management’s ability to strike great deals.”

Catalyst’s stock has since underperformed the S&P 500 index (SPY) by a score of roughly +19% to +10%.

In the article that follows, I discuss some recent developments that support a more neutral stance.

Recent Developments

A lot has happened in the past year for Catalyst.

First and foremost, in January, Catalyst settled with Teva. Teva received a license to market generic FIRDAPSE no earlier than 2035. In August, Catalyst reached a similar agreement with Lupin, another generic drug manufacturer. Effectively, the FIRDAPSE LOE risk I highlighted last year has been deferred for roughly a decade, barring an adverse outcome in the remaining Hetero litigation (a trial is expected for March 2026).

Why all the drama, though?

Recall that FIRDAPSE was first FDA-approved for adults with LEMS in November 2018. Orphan exclusivity ended in November 2025. However, Catalyst has multiple method-of-use (MOU) and dosing patents (Orange Book) that extend to 2037. However, MOU and dosing patents aren’t as “solid” as composition-of-matter patents on a new molecule. Under Hatch-Waxman, ANDA filers have to either wait until those patents expire or file a Paragraph IV challenge claiming the patents are invalid/not infringed and litigate. That’s what Teva, Lupin, and Hetero have done. Rather than litigate to a “final judgment,” Catalyst and Teva/Lupin have “shaken hands.” Of course, Hetero remains a “wild card,” but the largest competitors are contractually locked out until 2035.

These developments were critical because FIRDAPSE makes up ~60% of Catalyst’s total revenues. FIRDAPSE 2025 revenue guidance was reaffirmed between $355 million and $360 million.

Turning to AGAMREE, its launch is outpacing my expectations. Recall that I argued that AGAMREE was unlikely to approach EMFLAZA’s ~$255 million 2023 sales given limited differentiation and generic prednisone pressure. EMFLAZA, too, has a generic version available. Now, both EMFLAZA and AGAMREE are generally preferred over prednisone due to a better side effect profile. In Q3 2025, AGAMREE net revenue rose 115% Y/Y to $32.4 million. Catalyst is guiding for $105 million to $115 million for the full-year 2025.

While AGAMREE’s strength is real, depth is the next test. During the Q3 call, management stated that all top 45 DMD centers have at least one AGAMREE patient. Most of those patients came from either prednisone or from EFLAZA. So, AGAMREE’s tolerability narrative is really resonating with the DMD community. For now, payers are cooperative, with over 85% reimbursement success. That said, the key “swing factor” over the next few years is whether payers eventually push harder on step therapy versus cheaper steroids.

Meanwhile, FYCOMPA is clearly ex-growth. While there is only one generic so far, with a small discount (Teva’s generic is ~17% below WAC), more are expected to arrive, causing “additional erosion.” Despite this, FYCOMPA 2025 revenue guidance was raised to between $100 million and $110 million.

Strategy

We have to recognize Catalyst for what it is and isn’t. It isn’t a classic growth biotech. As I’ve pointed out in the past, the company doesn’t have its own “drug machine” and allocates very little to R&D (just $2.657 million in Q3 despite $148.392 million in total revenues). Catalyst looks more like a high-margin specialty pharma compounder.

In lieu of internal R&D, Catalyst always has an eye open for deals (in-licensing drugs, acquisitions, co-development, and so on) and new indications.

Regarding the latter, management hinted towards an oncology-LEMS exploration for FIRDAPSE. Earlier this year, the NCCN explicitly called out LEMS as a paraneoplastic syndrome in SCLC and specifically lists FIRDAPSE as the recommended symptomatic treatment.

While this represents a new market for FIRDAPSE, the addressable patient pool is intrinsically small and limited by SCLC incidence and survival (median survival often under 2 years). For instance, it is estimated that ~3% of SCLC (U.S. incidence of ~25-30k/year) have LEMS. So, this is several hundred patients. While oncology-LEMS can support modest growth going forward, I wouldn't expect a major boost from it. Another challenge is that many of these patients go undiagnosed. Although NCCN efforts increase awareness, this remains a commercial hurdle for FIRDAPSE in oncology-LEMS.

Financial Health

Catalyst has reportedly mulled over ~100 business development, or BD, assessments, but hasn’t pulled the trigger on anything lately. Instead, opting for a $200 million buyback through December 31, 2026. As of September 30, 2025, Catalyst had $689.9 million in cash and equivalents with no material long-term debt.

Q3 2025 total revenue came in at $148.4 million, up ~15% Y/Y, driven by FIRDAPSE ($92.2 million, +16% Y/Y), AGAMREE ($32.4 million, +115% Y/Y), and FYCOMPA ($23.8 million, -26% Y/Y). GAAP net income was $52.8 million, up 20% Y/Y.

Valuation Assumptions

Per my DCF model, CPRX appears undervalued on a cash flow basis.

For assumptions, I went with 5% "Revenue Growth," or CAGR (terminating at 2% after the nine-year projection) and a 35% "FCF Margin." This is consistent with CPRX’s recent performance.

"Year 1 Revenue" represents the consensus revenue estimate for 2026. Since we are still one quarter away from 2026, the "Fraction of Year Elapsed" was adjusted to -0.25 to ensure that cash flows are being properly discounted. My 1.4 "Beta" is a modeling estimate that reflects CPRX’s risk profile. The company is predominantly dependent on just two products and has no internal R&D engine.

Most everything else is either standard DCF modeling methodology or was pulled from CPRX’s latest financial statements (e.g., 10-Q).

Based on my assumptions, CPRX is undervalued by ~26%.

Note that my "Yearly Projections" are roughly in line with the consensus revenue estimates:

When I perform a “reverse DCF” via my Sensitivity Analysis, it’s clear that the market is pricing CPRX for much lower growth.

It is also possible that while future cash flows (within the modeling period) are relatively assured, there may be great uncertainty surrounding CPRX’s “tail value,” or terminal value.

While the market’s view is relatively conservative, I wouldn’t flag it as ridiculous. It appears that the market is pricing the stock as if FIRDAPSE eventually plateaus, FYCOMPA erodes rapidly as more generics enter, and AGAMREE growth slows once the first big wave of DMD center conversions is done and payers tighten. Margins likely compress from ~40% due to higher R&D and/or BD spend.

Basically, CPRX may be a good investment if it supports mid-single-digit revenue growth for a decade, FCF margin in the mid-30s, IP holds, and management doesn’t destroy value with BD. This will likely be “easier said than done.”

Final Words

Catalyst definitely merits a rating upgrade to Hold. Its narrative has shifted from “looming LOE” (although still relevant to FYCOMPA) into a conservatively-priced, "cash-rich specialty pharma with two durable pillars” (FIRDAPSE and AGAMREE).

That said, this is a business dependent on just two products with bare minimum R&D. So, CPRX stock remains dependent on management’s ability to execute disciplined BD rather than discovery.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Has A New Buyer, A Different Kind Of Central Bank
data.description:
In October, I wrote about the structural changes in the buyers of gold, and attributed much of the rise in the price of gold to ex-U.S. forces, namely central banks and the speculation of de-dollarizing economies (which is likely folly, but I don't dictate what other countries do).

Recent data has come out that changes my perspective on where the gold demand is coming from (it's still not the United States!), and it has made me more bullish than I was before. The structural changes are still underway.

Foreign Central Bank Demand Is Still Strong

Don't get me wrong here; I still believe that the major drivers of long-term gold demand are coming from outside the United States.

We know that it's not U.S. institutions (well, they are speculating, but not really allocating). We can see that in conservative institutions like family offices, allocation to gold is abysmally low.

I covered this in "Smart Money Is Reallocating." Here's my favorite chart from that article. It shows family office average allocation changes from the last two years.

Even though alternatives are on the rise, this hasn't included commodities. U.S. institutions are still eschewing direct gold exposure, despite its doubling in value over that time.

But foreign central banks, notably the People's Bank of China ("PBOC"), have been importing gold like nobody's business. PBOC's share of gold in its reserves is now the highest in modern history.

I detailed these purchases in October, but it's notable that since then, we've only seen further increases in purchases. Of course, this data is a little noisy, because it's based on IMF reporting, and some countries tend not to report fully (looking at you, PBOC). However, the trend remains intact for the year, and sales of gold have dropped off despite the paper profits many of these central banks are sitting on.

Following September, October set a new YTD record on purchases from central banks across the world. This is still below the November 2024 spike, which I believe was a response to the U.S. election.

But central banks are not speculators, and they are often more concerned with how much gold they have on hand than how much it's worth. This is less true for the new player on the scene, which had been buying gold before now but recently ratcheted up their efforts: cryptocurrency issuers.

Securing Stablecoins With Gold

Those in the cryptocurrency space will likely recognize the ticker USDT-USD, often just called "Tether." It's named after the company that issues it, and it is the world's largest stablecoin. These are cryptocurrencies chained to the value of a dollar. One USDT should always carry the same value as one dollar, and since we price the asset in dollars (or at least I do as an American), that should make the currency interchangeable with real dollars. Crypto skeptics will argue otherwise, but that is for another article.

When I first heard reports of Tether buying gold, this made a lot of sense to me. They also issue a cryptocurrency tied to the price of gold, XAUT-USD, and as they issue more of that coin, they need to buy the requisite amount of gold to back up the value.

But now, Tether has more gold on hand than the Shanghai Futures Exchange. This is a huge shift, and it happened quickly.

I knew that USDT was not entirely backed by dollars. Instead, it's backed by a mix of reserves, mostly dollars and deposits, but also Bitcoin and gold ("precious metals"). What's interesting is that this rush for gold wasn't just going to back XAUT issuance; most of it went to USDT's balance sheet.

From Q2 to Q3 this year, they increased their precious metals holdings on USDT's balance sheet from $8 billion to nearly $13 billion. That's a big jump in just one quarter.

Here are their current assets as of the last quarterly report:

When we compare this to central bank demand, we're starting to see Tether alone take up more and more of the total demand. Keep in mind, the "central banks" bar is all the world's central banks combined, including the PBOC.

What's most fascinating is when you see Tether compared to these central banks individually. It really shows how staggering this recent accumulation has been. This is just for Q3 this year.

It's Unlikely That Others Will Follow

Here's the tricky part. Immediately, I think there will be many people who jump to -- well, if Tether's doing this, then demand for gold from other stablecoin issuers like the second-largest USDC-USD, issued by Circle (CRCL) should follow.

There's a wrench in the plans. One named "GENIUS." Passed earlier this year, the Genius Act requires that U.S.-based stablecoin issuers have full 1:1 reserves backing the stablecoins they issue, and these reserves can only be held in deposits and in Treasuries.

Here is the relevant text from the bill:

(1) In general.—A permitted payment stablecoin issuer shall—

(A) maintain identifiable reserves backing the outstanding payment stablecoins of the permitted payment stablecoin issuer on an at least 1 to 1 basis, with reserves comprising—

(i) United States coins and currency (including Federal Reserve notes) or money standing to the credit of an account with a Federal Reserve Bank;

(ii) funds held as demand deposits (or other deposits that may be withdrawn upon request at any time) or insured shares at an insured depository institution (including any foreign branches or agents, including correspondent banks, of an insured depository institution), subject to limitations established by the Corporation and the National Credit Union Administration, as applicable, to address safety and soundness risks of such insured depository institution;

(iii) Treasury bills, notes, or bonds—

(I) with a remaining maturity of 93 days or less; or

(II) issued with a maturity of 93 days or less;

(iv) money received under repurchase agreements, with the permitted payment stablecoin issuer acting as a seller of securities and with an overnight maturity, that are backed by Treasury bills with a maturity of 93 days or less;

(V) reverse repurchase agreements, with the permitted payment stablecoin issuer acting as a purchaser of securities and with an overnight maturity, that are collateralized by Treasury notes, bills, or bonds on an overnight basis, subject to overcollateralization in line with standard market terms...

This means that U.S.-based issuers will be unable to hold any gold on their balance sheet. Could they issue gold-backed coins like XAUT-USD? Absolutely. But this demand may be isolated to Tether since they are the only large stablecoin issuer outside the United States.

That being said, Tether makes up 60% of the stablecoin market globally. So even though other issuers won't follow this change and the strategic shift from Tether is very bullish for gold, I don't see a world in which the purchases slow down any time soon given how stablecoins are becoming more and more popular.

BRICS Currency Speculation

All of this doesn't account for the fact that I believe in the next five years we will see a BRICS-centric payment system that relies on a basket of precious metals (of which gold will likely dominate).

For the record, BRICS is an informal economic alliance between Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa, and the economies aligned to them ("BRICS+").

Before I get any comments of you're crazy, nothing can ever replace the dollar, I doubt that a BRICS currency would actually be aimed as a global dollar replacement but as a means of dollar avoidance by the specific economies in question.

I don't even think it would be used in the countries that are part of BRICS. A BRICS currency would not be designed to replace the Renminbi, Rupee, Real, Ruble, Rand, etc. (first time I've realized that all the BRICS currencies start with R), from my view. Instead, it would be used as a medium between these countries and likely between oil producers in OPEC.

To get the Saudis and Emirates to de-dollarize, something that China has long wanted, they would need something that is as trusted as the dollar, since the BRICS currencies mentioned above are not as trustworthy as the dollar due to devaluation and authoritarian practices in the governments in regard to fiat currencies.

A currency backed by a basket of precious metals and redeemable for those precious metals at the central banks, like the PBOC, could make a lot of sense.

There just isn't enough gold for it to be entirely gold-backed. But if we added silver, platinum, and palladium to the mix, things start to check out a little more. Look at where these metals are mined. Of special note is the monopoly BRICS has on platinum and palladium mining.

Like I said, I don't think this is a replacement for domestic currencies, but rather it will be mostly digitally transferred as a means of having a hard currency that can pay for oil, which is currently almost completely dollarized.

U.S. sanctions on Lukoil and Rosneft only complicate this because they're forcing China and India away from Russian oil and into the dollarized trade coming out of the Middle East. Over time, I expect the dollar paradigm to be challenged, and that will be a significant catalyst for the prices of most precious metals, gold in particular.

This is not something that will happen immediately, but slowly and quietly. The indicator to me that it has begun is the rise in central bank purchases in the first place, especially the PBOC, which ramped up gold purchases right after we weaponized the dollar and froze $300 billion in Russian assets following their invasion of Ukraine.

How I'm Buying Gold Exposure

I am still very bullish on gold, mostly for the reasons I've outlined here and in my last report. The structural shifts in demand are not coming from the United States, and U.S. institutions still just look like speculators. This is changing quickly, and shifts like that of Tether are very indicative that not only are central banks hoarding gold, but now so are cryptocurrency issuers. That opens up a whole new lane for gold demand.

Currently, my largest gold-related holding has become GDMN, which I covered last month. It is a fund that owns both gold and global gold miners. This is what I recommend to most people because it allows you to take a smaller stance and get more exposure to both gold and the miners that dig it up, which often can rise in value faster than gold itself during these bullish cycles.

Conclusion

I see the structural demand for gold as growing, and this includes not only central banks but now also another kind of central bank: cryptocurrency issuers. Gold is being bought up as part of a means of backing USDT, which makes up 60% of the stablecoin market. This is a huge shift, and one that shouldn't be ignored.

Of course, speculation is still rampant, and gold's recent volatility has proven that while U.S. investors are not accumulating and allocating to gold like those in the rest of the world, they are still trading it heavily. I expect that volatility to continue moving forward, but that is not necessarily a bad thing. It does warrant caution.

As always, and especially with gold now, this line is prescient:

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
